The growth, liquor and activity corresponds to the period of gestation. We have noted there is bilateral talipes deformity (CTEV). Movements of both the lower limbs appeared normal.   The etiology of this condition could be multifactorial usually , chromosomal abnormality or genetic ( less common )  . Absolute chromosomal normalcy can be established only by direct testing (Amniocentesis for QPCR /CMA /DNA store).  Single gene disorder in its full spectrum can be evaluated postnatally only.  Treatment of CTEV would mainly involve physiotherapy and orthopedic supports.Some cases may require surgical correction. Treatment has to start soon after birth and therefore it would be advisable to seek an orthopedic opinion during the antenatal period itself, to understand the extent of correction and outcomes. Delivery management need not change because of this condition per se.  Suggested : 1) Amniocentesis  for QFPCR /CMA/DNA store. 2) Consultation with paediatric orthopaedician to plan postnatal management. 3) Detailed postnatal clinical evaluation of the baby and genetic counselling. Monochorionic Triamniotic Triplets. gestation corresponding to a gestational age of 12 Weeks Fetus A,B,C - Intrauterine Gestational age assigned as per LMP  Fetus - A Placenta - Common placenta seen anteriorly Liquor - Normal  Obliterated intracranial translucency (IT) Sacral meningomyelocele ---- suggestive of Open neural tube defect ( ONTD)   Fetus - B Placenta - Common placenta seen anteriorly Liquor - Normal  Fetus - C Placenta - Common placenta seen anteriorly Liquor - Normal  Maternal: Bilateral hyperstimulated ovaries.  Counseling: Primigravida  Blood group O+tive  This is a Monochorionic triamniotic triplet gestation corresponding to 12 weeks . The issues associated with monochorionic gestation in specific and triplet gestation in general were discussed with them.Fetus A with open neural tube defect would have a guarded prognosis postnatally. This condition would need postnatal surgical care . There would be long term morbidity related to motor, sensory, neurological issues and bowel/ bladder continence. Prognosis for fetus A is guarded / less favourable.   a: Triplet gestation:There is a 75% risk of preterm delivery before 34 weeks in uncomplicated monochorionic triplets(60% for triplets overall) . Close maternal monitoring    for maternal blood pressure/ glycemic control has been informed.   b: Monochorionic gestation: All monochorionic gestations have a risk of developing selective growth restriction/ Twin to twin transfusion syndrome and rarely Twin anemia polycythemia sequence.    The same needs to be monitored by USG evaluating the growth/ liquor/bladder/ doppler of all the fetuses - 2 weekly.  At present, fetus B /C are concordant and fetus A has open neural tube defect which has been explained to them .  c.Management options :   -- Review at 16 weeks for early target Microwave ablation of one of the fetuses as it is a monochorionic gestation. However, the procedure per se, would have a 20-25% procedure related risk of miscarriage / Preterm labour / Cotwin anaemia & associated issues. Also the chance of development of TTTS between the remaining fetuses cannot be excluded post procedure . Any such intervention also is best done after the 16th week only. -- Conservative Care - serial monitoring 2 weekly from 16 weeks to look for MCTA related complications and to manage the fetus with ONTD after birth   Suggested repeat scan at 16-17 weeks for early target scan. (Appointment fixed on 13/6/25 at  11.00 am in I room , Mediscan , Mylapore)   In case of any queries, you can reach us at 044-24663240 / 044-24663168 / genetics@mediscan.org.in

We have noted a small echogenic lesion - right lung - suggestive of possible CPAM/BPS.  CPAM, is a developmental abnormality of the lung which encompases a broad category of problems. With ongoing pregnancy the size of the lesion can remain same, regress in most .In some it may increase.  Prognosis in these cases would depend on the progression of the lesion and development of fetal hydrops. An indicator for the same being the CVR(CPAM/Volume ratio). At present the CVR is <1.6 & there are no features of hydrops which indicates lesser risk of imminent problem. This pregnancy would need to be monitored 2 weekly to look for 1. CPAM volume 2. features of hydrops 3. Hydramnios.  We have seen that these lesions usually plateau by 26-28 weeks and do not increase in size further and hence close monitoring in this period is essential.  We have also explained the postnatal implications of the problem. Lesions that regress/ not being large & no hydrops, generally are associated with favourable outcomes. Some babies would need postnatal management of  the lesion and nature of the same would depend on followup visits & postnatal findings. Today's scan findings and implications have been explained in detail to the couple.   In today's scan, we have noted above mentioned findings suggestive of Left sided diaphragmatic hernia with left lobe of liver, stomach and bowel as contents. There is also single umbilical artery noted. Cardiac examination revealed PLSVC with asymmetry of chambers and arches and ARSA. Occult anomalies of GIT/GUT if present can be detected only postnatally.   Congenital diaphragmatic hernia is a condition caused by a developmental defect in the diaphragm. The etiology for this condition can be multifactorial, chromosomal / genetic.   This condition in-utero generally does not pose any problems to the fetus, however as pregnancy advances, there may be a risk of polyhydramnios which could increase the risk of preterm labour.  Prognosis generally depends upon the gestational age at onset of the problem, contents, Observed to expected lung-head ratio (O/E LHR) at later gestation, gestational age at delivery, presence of associated problems and the postnatal lung compliance. In this fetus, prognosis is guarded due to early gestational age at onset and presence of large portion of liver in the thorax. We have explained regarding postnatal respiratory sequelae and the need for surgery postnatallly.   Evaluation of current pregnancy is suggested to understand the cause which will also help in counseling for their subsequent pregnancy. We would suggest direct testing - Amniocentesis - QFPCR/ CMA/ DNA store after 16 weeks. Pros, cons and risks of the procedure and turn around time of the results explained to the couple. QFPCR report will be available in one week and CMA in 3-4 weeks. Single gene disorders cannot be determined in entire extent prenatally.    After counseling, couple were keen on continuing the pregnancy.  Suggested : 1. Review at 16-17 weeks for early target scan and plan direct testing - Amniocentesis - QFPCR/ CMA/ DNA store. Appointment for the same has been tentatively fixed on 10/06/25 at 10.00 a.m., J room, Mediscan, Mylapore Contact Ph.No.044-24663240).  2. In the unfortunate event of fetal demise or in case the couple change their decision and plan to discontinue pregnancy, we would suggest that the fetus and placenta be sent in 10 % formalin (9 parts of water and 1 part of formalin) for a detailed autopsy and HPE (Ph.No.044-24663260/261). This will help us in confirming the diagnosis as well as to rule out other anomalies that may not be evident on ultrasound. Information thus gained would help them prior to their subsequent pregnancies. Labetalol 100 mg - Overt DM on T.Metformin and Insulin - Hypothyroidism on T.Thyronorm 12 mcg  Aneuploidy screening : First trimester screening -  Bhcg  -  0.37MoM, Pappa - 0.66 MoM, NT1  -  1.1 MoM, Nasal bone present,  NT2 - 0.82 MoM, Nasal bone present,  Age risk - 1:101 Twin1 - T18- 1 :816, T13 - 1 :2365, T21 - 1:1466 Twin2 - T18- 1 :2141,  T13 - 1 :9139, T21 - 1:8286  Mrs.Madhavi Kamala Kannan was referred to us for second opinion for fetus B for Bilateral Congenital Talipes Equino Varus. Their's is a dichorionic diamniotic pregnancy. Labelling of the twins done according to the standard protocol. Twin close to cervix is labelled as Twin A.  The scan findings and its implications has been explained to the couple in detail.  Fetus A is 21% smaller in growth compared to Fetus B . However both fetuses growth ar within normal centiles and dopplers are normal. No major structural malformations are noted for the period of pregnancy for fetus A.  On detailed anatomical evaluation of fetus B - Bilateral CTEV is noted. Muscle mass of the lower extremites appeared normal and flexion and extension noted at both hip and knee joints are normal. No other major anomalies/ no markers for aneuploidy are seen.  The etiology of talipes can be multifactorial causes or due to chromosomal, genetic etiology  . Absolute chormosomal normalcy in any pregnancy can only be established by direct testing by amniocentesis QF-PCR, Chromosomal microarray and DNA store.The pros,cons and procedure related risk and the turn around time for each result have been explained to the couple. Single gene disorders cannot be determined in its entire spectrum prenatally. In some, bilateral CTEV can be secondary to evolving musculoskeletal abnormality. Hence this pregnancy need serial monitoring for assessment of fetal limb movements. Talipes deformity , if isolated , usually has a favourable outcome.  Treatment of CTEV involves physiotherapy and orthopedic supports. Some children may require surgical correction.Treatment has to start soon after birth and therefore it would be advisable to seek an orthopedic opinion during the antenatal period itself. Delivery management need not change because of this condition per se.   Plan : 1)Amniocentesis for fetus A/B- for QFPCR/ Chromosomal microarray and DNA store . Considering hydropic fetus , high risk of adverse outcomes / intra uterine fetal demise / neonatal demise has been explained . As the gestational age is around 30 weeks , preterm delivery is not advised.   We have offered following in utero management options to the family - 1. Serial aspiration of pleural fluid - to prevent pulmonary hypoplasia and improve ventilation / chances of survival after birth . 2. Thoraco amniotic shunt - involves inserting small catheter from pleural space on one side into the amniotic sac allowing continuous drainage of fluid to relieve pressure leading to hydrops . Benefits ( prevent cardiac compromise / pulmonary hypoplasia to improve survival)and risks ( PROM , preterm labour , shunt dislogment / blockage, infections , need for reshunting ) have been briefed .  We may need to shunt the other side also , if it does not drain fully from the other side. The need for close follow up with serial ultrasounds and doppler studies is advised.  Prognosis remains guarded and outcome will depend on the cause , gestational age at delivery and neonatal clinical status after birth .  Hence delivery needs to be planned in tertiary care centre with level 3 NICU facilities .

Dichorionic triamniotic triplets gestation corresponding to a gestational age of 13 Weeks Fetus A,B,C - Intrauterine Fetus A and Fetus B - Monochorionic Diamniotic pair Gestational age assigned by as per BT Date (19/03/2025) EDD by BT 05/12/2025  Fetus - A Placenta - Common placenta seen posteriorly Liquor - Normal  Fetus - B Placenta - Common placenta seen posteriorly Liquor - Normal  Fetus - C Placenta - Anterior Liquor - Normal  Counseling: Gravida : Primi Blood group : B+tive  Counseling: The ultrasound findings have been explained to the couple. They have been referred as DCDA twins for first trimester scan. Today's scan shows dichorionic triamniotic triplet gestation. Fetus - A & B have posterior placenta/ Monochorionic diamniotic pair . Fetus -C have anterior placenta/ Separate fetus.  All fetuses are concordant for CRL and structural anatomy at this period of gestation.  Triplet pregnancy have a slightly higher risk of pregnancy complications in terms of preterm, premature rupture of membrane, cervical incompetence, maternal pregnancy associated medical complications. Moreover, monochorionic twins in view of common placenta and vascular anastomosis have a  15% risk of complications- selective fetal growth restriction/ twin to twin transfusion syndrome/ twin anaemia polycythemia sequence which may become evident only on serial scan after 16 weeks.  Hence the following options were discussed 1) Conservative management and follow-up/ serial monitoring of triplets from 16 weeks for serial  evaluation from 16 wks and 2 weekly thereafter to asses monochorionic twins. 2) Selective fetal reduction- a)  Reduction of MCDA pair & continuation as singleton : This would avoid risk of monochorionic diamniotic complications and offer better pregnancy outcome with respect to live birth and near term delivery. Singleton pregnancy have to be assesed in detail at 16 weeks and 20 weeks  for fetal anatomy and follow-up for growth scan.   b) Reduction of singleton & continuation of MCDA pair : As discussed earlier, since MCDA pair have the above mentioned concerns in some, they need to be monitored at a dedicated twin unit from 16 weeks, 2 weekly to asses the growth / liquor/ bladder & fetal dopplers, referred appropriately for any management if needed.   Pros, cons, risks of procedure have been explained. This has a 5-7% procedure related risk of miscarriage.      Couple inquired for monochorionic pair selective reduction of one of the twin . The option regards the same - microwave ablation or intrafetal laser photocoagulation. However risk of loss of co-twin MCDA is higher in reported data(close to 30-50%) and there would also be increased procedure related risk to pregnancy as a whole. Hence, we would not recommend this as a primary option.  After counselling , we have asked them to discuss with you and revert back. Reduction from twins to singleton is done in case of major structural anomaly of one twin or for maternal indication - maternal medical/surgical history that contraindicates twin gestation. 3. The couple are willing for reduction to singleton without aneuploidy screening in view of advanced maternal age and maternal medical history of diabetes and hypertension . Cause - No testing done  G3- Present pregnancy / NIPT - low risk   Mrs. Rekha Kamalakannan was referred to us for target scan with fetal echocardiography at 21 weeks 4 days. In today's scan, fetal activity and liquor appeared normal. Assessment of the fetal heart showed an echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle. Detailed fetal echocardiography has shown a structurally normal fetal heart.   Fetus shows Congenital pulmonary airway malformation (CPAM)  of left lung, with mild displacement of heart to the right. Right lung appears normal. No evidence of hydrops at present.Left lateral ventricle prominent measuring 8.4mm. Intracranial anatomy normal . The same to be reviewed on followup .   CPAM is a developmental abnormality of the lung. The lesion generally plateu by 26-28 weeks, hence 2 weekly monitoring upto that time is suggested to assess the size and CVR volume. Most of the CPAM regress in the third trimester, rarely some can remain static or increase in size. Postnatally the newborn needs to be examined by pediatrician to look for any respiratory symptoms. Based on followup in childhood and lung imaging , some children may need surgical resection of the lesion. Outcomes are generally favourble.   Suggested repeat scan after 2 weeks to assess size and CVR volume. Today's scan we have noted Dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twins . There is two separate placenta and thick membrane seen in between suggestive of DCDA twins. Labelling of twins done according to standard protocol.  Todays scan both fetus corresponds to 24 week 1 day gestation with normal liqour and activity. Today's scan findings and limitations of advanced gestational age have been explained to the patient, husband and her mother in detail.  Fetus A shows normal growth. Fetus  B - growth parameters along lower centiles - small for gestational age.  Bladder is seen in both fetuses with normal liquor in both.   Assessment of the fetal heart of Fetus A showed an echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle. Detailed fetal echocardiography has shown a structurally normal fetal heart. Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in left ventricle is commonly seen as a normal variant. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  We have noted Unossified nasal bone in fetus B. On reviewing her history we have noted they have not done first trimester screening. Scan findings and screening test and its implications has been explained to them.   Unossified nasal bone can be a normal variant or a marker of aneuploidy like trisomy 21. This increase apriori risk for aneuploidy by 6 fold.  Absolute chromosomal normalcy can be diagnosed by direct testing (amniocentesis - QFPCR / Karyotype ) only for both fetuses. Pros and cons of the test have been explained. If the KT is normal, routine pregnancy care can be continued. If the KT is abnormal for one fetus, further management of pregnancy by option of selective fetal reduction  can be done at that juncture based on parental decision . Unossified nasal bone would not have any postnatal implications in the absence of downs syndrome.  Suggested: 1) Amniocentesis for QF PCR / KT for both fetus. QF PCR report will be available in 1 week time and KT report will be available in 3 weeks time. In todays scan,the growth,liquor and activity is normal Lateral ventricle size within normal limits. Fetus shows CPAM left  lung with mild mediastinal shift.  Rest of left lung and right lung normal.Rest of fetus appeared normal. No evidence of hydrops at present  CPAM, is a developmental abnormality of the lung . There is no chromosomal association with this problem and hence at present no further testing is warranted.  Predominently these lesions tend to regress with favourable outcome.The size of the lesion can increase in some cases leading to hydrops.Prognosis in these cases would depend on the progression of the lesion indicator for the same being the CVR(CPAM/Volume ratio). At present the CVR is 0.44 (<1.6 favourable outcome) & there are no features of hydrops which indicates lesser risk of imminent problem. .  This pregnancy would need to be monitored after 2 weeks to look for 1. CPAM volume 2. features of hydrops 3. Hydramnios. We have seen that these lesions usually plateau by third trimester and regress by term and tend to have favourable outcome. Fetal findings explained to the couple. Today's scan findings and its implications explained to the couple in detail  In todays scan, the growth, liquor and activity is normal. We have noted cleft lip and palate on right side and single umbilical artery. Rest of fetus appeared structurally normal for the period of gestation  Patient is known case of overt DM with high HBA1c during early pregnancy (During period of organogenesis). Hence the etiology of facial cleft could be due to maternal overt DM or due to single gene disorder or chromosomal abnormality. In order to ascertain the genetic etiology, we would advise Direct testing ( Amniocentesis) for QFPCR/ CMA ( Chromosomal microarray )/DNA store. Single gene disorders in its entirety can only be determined postnatally.  Evolving anomalies if any  will be evident in the subsequent scans. The possibility of developing polyhydramnios inview of CLCP and risk of preterm delivery have also been explained. Certain occult anomalies of the GIT (imperforate anus/  tracheoesophageal fistula)/ GUT can be identified only after birth.  Isolated CLCP is a potentially correctable problem that requires postnatal treatment. Post natally baby will require supportive treatment, palatal obturators and multiple step-wise surgeries for correction of the cleft lip and palate, as well as orthodontic treatment. The nature and timing of the surgery/ies and long term outcomes would depend upon the findings at postnatal evaluation by plastic surgeon- usually cleft lip is operated at 3 months age and cleft palate at 9 months age. The child may also require multi disciplinary care involving plastic surgery, ENT, dental and speech therapy.  We suggest to have an antenatal consultation with Dr Syed Altaf, Plastic surgeon, for better understanding of the condition and post natal management.  Suggested : 1) If the QFPCR / CMA/ DNA store, If willing Contact Ph.No.044-24663240 2) Serial maternal BP Monitoring and serial fetal growth monitoring, in view of high uterine artery PI and Low PAPPA ( 0.2 MoM) 3) Review scan at 28 weeks for reassesment and growth scan(appointment fixed on 11/8/2025  at  11.00  am , FMU , Mediscan , Mylapore)  4) Antenatal consutation with plastic surgeon Dr Syed Altaf.(Referral letter enclosed) 5) Delivery in a tertiary care centre with NICU facilities. 6) Detailed Postnatal clinical examination of the newborn. In today's scan we have noted the above findings. The donor twin (Fetus B) has reduced liquor with maximum vertical pocket 2.4 cm and bladder is seen. The recipient(Fetus A) twin has polyhydramnios with the maximum vertical pool of 12.0 cms with bladder seen. Both fetuses shows normal growth and fetal activity with growth discordancy of 11% for Fetus B. The recepient twin (Fetus A) shows high resistance flow in umbilical artery and normal flow in ductus venosus. In Fetus B, the umbilical artery / ductus venosus dopplers show normal flow pattern. Cardiac imaging of fetus A(recepient) shows mild cardiac dysfunction with a CHOP SCORE of 4. Part of common anterior placenta for recepient (Twin A) appears thin and hypoechoic whereas part of common anterior placenta for Donor (Twin B) appears thick and hyperechoic. MCA PSV falls at 0.73 MoM for Fetus A (Recepient) and 1.27 MoM for Fetus B (Donor). The above features are suggestive of Twin to Twin Transfusion Syndrome (TTTS) Stage I with Twin Anemia Polycythemia Sequence (TAPS) - Stage I The findings were explained to the patient, her husband and her mother in law. We have explained that these changes are due to vascular anastomoses in the placenta. The imbalance in the flow across these vessels results in liquor discordancy in these monochorionic twins.   The treatment options which is ideal would be a Fetoscopic Selective laser coagulation of anastamotic vessels was discussed in detail with the couple and their family. The nature of the procedure, technical issues, and the procedure related complications (PROM/Preterm labour/Miscarriage/IUFD of one or both twins/ Post laser TAPS) were explained to them in detail.   However , in view of the unusual cord insertion sites / co-existant TAPS resulting in a bulky placenta on one side/ liquor in donor sac -  prenatal intervention by fetoscopic laser may not be technically feasible at this stage.   Hence after extensive discussion , at present following plan is discussed - Fetal blood sampling for both the fetuses  - Review with reports , to plan for therapeutic amniodrainage  - Serial monitoring for weekly review of the cardiac status / doppler of recepient - Further decision of pregnancy care/ subsequent intervention based on the evolving changes  The fact that , these are temporary measures which may need subsequent therapy has been discussed   In case of any queries please contact Ph.No.044-24663168 / 240, Whatsapp No: 9094533300 eMail ID : fts@mediscan.org.in

Theirs is a DCDA twin gestation. Fetus A has posterior placenta and fetus B has anterior placenta. Growth , liquour and structural anatomy is normal for the period of gestation for fetus A & B. We have noted that fetus B has situs inversus totalis. Detailed fetal echocardiography done for both the fetus shows a structurally normal heart. Situs inversus totalis, in some cases is associated with ciliopathy related disorders. In most instances, in the setting of normal structural anatomy and normal intracardiac anatomy, it has good long term prognosis and no postnatal treatment is required for the same. In the event associated with ciliopathy, there are chances of recurrent respiratory tract infections after birth. Prenatal testing of ciliopathy can be done by amniotic fluid sampling by Whole Exome Sequencing. Pros, cons, risks related to the procedure and yield of the test was explained. Results of the test may not change the mode of pregnancy care and the same can be evaluated postnatally also .  The couple has been reassured of the above and routine 4 weekly follow up as for any DCDA twins is advised for growth & cardiac assessment and postnatal evaluation of fetus B.  Suggested: 1) 4 weekly follow up scan for growth & cardiac reassessment. Mild renal pelvicaliectasis regress spontaneously in majority of the cases as gestation advances. However, in some cases, it may remain static or increase. Hence followup is advised. Pelviectasis is a weak marker for aneuploidy and after incorporating the same, posterior risk for Down's syndrome is 1 in 207.   In view of advanced maternal age with a soft marker on ultrasound we have discussed the following options for further evaluation:  1) Direct testing by amniocentesis for QFPCR/KT - Absolute chromosomal normalcy can be determined only by direct testing by amniocentesis for QFPCR/KT. Pros, cons and risks of the procedure explained. QFPCR report will be available in one week and karyotype in 3-4 weeks. This is the preferred option considering advanced maternal age.  2) Non invasive prenatal screening (NIS): This is a screening test based on cell free fetal DNA in maternal blood with a detection rate of 99% for Down's syndrome. A screen positive or no call result would however require direct testing for confirmation. Limitations of screening/ testing in advanced gestation explained,   After counseling, couple opted out of the tests mentioned above and opted for routine follow up of the pregnancy. Mild dilatation of renal pelviectasis noted in right kidney. No other major structural abnormalities are noted for the period of gestation.Minimal renal pelviectasis regress mostly by 3rd trimester. In some cases, it may increase. Hence followup is advised.  Detailed fetal echocardiography has been done, which shows the presence of Aberrant Right Subclavian artery and echogenic intracardiac focus seen in left ventricle (normal variant).  Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in left ventricle is commonly seen as a normal variant. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  ARSA as such is not a cardiac defect and does not cause symptoms postnatally in the presence of normal aortic arch. It remains a benign entity in majority, but there are reports mentioning it being a marker for certain chromosomal abnormalities in a smaller percentage of cases. As aneuploidy screening was not done in this fetus, only invasive testing can clarify on the possibility of any chromosomal abnormality.  Absolute chromosomal normalcy can be ascertained only by direct testing -  (Amniocentesis- QFPCR and CMA. The pros, cons and risks of the procedure has been explained to the couple in detail.  Chromosomal microarray is recommended for this patient to rule out 22q11.2 microdeletion as it is a common association with ARSA. The scan findings have been explained to the couple.  In today's scan, the fetus is growing well for the period of gestation with normal liquor and activity. We have noted left pelvic kidney. Right kidney and rest of the fetus is structurally normal.   At present, liquor and bladder is normal which is suggestive of preserved renal function. However,serial follow up assessment is advised to look for evolving obstructive changes in the kidneys(pelvicalyceal dilatation/cystic dysplasia).   Postnatal prognosis and outcomes are favourable in most. Few babies with renal anomalies can have associated occult anomalies (TEF/imperforate anus/genitourinary abnormalities) which cannot be picked up antenatally, hence postnatal evaluation and KUB is advised.   It was noted that she has not undergone any aneuploidy screening in the pregnancy. However, a screen positive/ "No call" test will need further testing for ascertaining chromosomal normalcy.  Absolute chromosomal normalcy in any pregnancy can only be established by direct testing - Amniocentesis .  Suggested:  1. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  On reviewing her history and as told by patient, First trimester screening test showed T21 risk as 1:381( Intermediate risk) and in view of intermediate risk and advanced maternal age, they suggested Amniocentesis and NIPT as options, they opted for NIPT and  gave sample for the same and report awaited. A positive screen test/ no call result in NIPT would require a direct test by amniocentesis for QFPCR/ KT to look for absolute chromosomal normalcy. QF PCR report will be available in 1-week time and KT report will be available in 3 weeks time.Pros, cons, risks of the procedure have been briefed.  The above details have been explained to the couple.  Suggested to review with NIPT at the earliest.

MONOCHORIONIC MONOAMNIOTIC TWIN PREGNANCY WITH TWIN REVERSED ARTERIAL PERFUSION SEQUENCE OF TWIN A (TRAP SEQUENCE)  COUNSELLING General history :  Married for 3 years, Non consanguinous marriage, Blood group : B Positive,Height = 154cms. Weight = 65 kgs.  Husband's blood group : A+ve  Obstetric history :  Primigravida  The above scan findings and its implications have been explained to the family in detail. The scan today shows twin gestation with a single large placenta and no intertwin membrane - suggestive of Monochorionic Monoamniotic twins. Fetus closer to cervix was labelled as fetus A and other fetus was labelled as Fetus B.   Fetus A: Acardiac edematous twin with rudimentary spine and lower limb and limited truncal development with single umbilical artery and color doppler showing reversed flow--suggestive of TRAP fetus (perfused twin)   Fetus B: Structurally normal fetus with normal doppler study at present --suggestive of PUMP fetus.   Fetus A (perfused twin) is an abnormal TRAP fetus being perfused by blood pumped from Fetus B through an abnormal arterio- arterial connections within the monochorionic placenta. The volume of TRAP fetus is large- 230 cc. With advancing gestation, if left untreated it could lead to cardiac overload for pump twin resulting in cardiac failure and intrauterine demise.   Hence, we have suggested them following plan:  Intervention for coagulation of vascular supply of the TRAP fetus by Microwave ablation/Interstitial laser to improve pregnancy outcomes. The details of the procedure have been explained to the family. Pros, cons, risks (Preterm delivery /PROM/Cotwin anaemia / IUFD) have been explained. Management in the setting of cotwin anemia has also been explained. Amniotic fluid sampling for KT/QFPCR would also be done at the same sitting  After counseling the couple opted for intervention and same has been scheduled for 23/01/2025. Post procedure advised to followup 2 weekly for review of growth / doppler of FETUS B  In case of any queries please contact Ph.No.044-24663168 / 240,  Whatsapp No:9094533300, eMail ID : fts@mediscan.org.in

She had done first trimester screening elsewhere which is suggestive of low risk for common aneuploidies and nasal bone included as present. Hence this report cannot be interpreted  In today's scan, fetus is growing well with normal liquor and activity. Fetus is structurally normal for the period of gestation and noted that Nasal bone is unossified. There were no other soft markers in today's scan.  Unossified nasal bone could be a normal variant or could be a marker for chromosomal aneuploidy - Down's syndrome. Absolute chromosomal normalcy can only be established by direct testing (QFPCR and Karyotype).  We have explained to them that in chromosomally normal fetuses, unossified nasal bone would have no postnatal implications.  In view of unossified nasal bone, we have discussed following options of common aneuploidy screening with them:   1) Non Invasive prenatal screening (NIS): This is a screening test done from cell free fetal DNA in maternal blood which provides 99% sensitivity for prediction of Down's syndrome. A 'no-call' or positive result would however require direct testing for confirmation.  2) Direst testing by Amniocentesis for QFPCR/KT/DNA store. Pros,cons, risk and turn over time of the procedure have been explained.  After counselling patinet opted for the direct testing-amniocentesis, fixed appointment on 24/1/2025.  Suggested: If test result is negative, repeat scan at 20-21 weeks for detailed target scan and fetal echocardiography. Todays scan fetus correspond to 32 weeks 6 days and fetus has normal growth, activity,liquor . Couple have opted out of counseling and consultation.  There is vein of galen malformation  and  evidence of hyperdynamic circulation. Vein of Galen malformation is a complex vascular anomaly which is developmental in origin, usually detected late in pregnancy. At present cardiac function is normal . Ductus venosus flow in normal which suggests compensated state.  In addition to these findings there is non visualisation of gall bladder . This finding has been noted at their prior visit. Isolated non visualisation of the gall bladder can be a transient finding / normal variant in most of the cases where they usually have normal outcome. Gall bladder can be visualised postnatally. In some it can be due to gall bladder agenesis which again would not have any perinatal implications. In a small percentage of cases of cystic fibrosis / biliary atresia also present with absent gall bladder. Neither of the above conditions can be ascertained/ruled out prenatally. A postnatal clinical assesment and pediatrician evaluation will help determine the same.  This finding would not have any prenatal implications.  Vein of Galen malformation would however be associated with cardiac dysfunction/ anaemia/ hydrops which can evolve in some. There would be need for postnatal neurosurgical intervention with the associated risks . Close prenatal monitoring with followup 2 weeks hence from now is advised. In the absence of any features of prenatal deterioration , delivery can be planned at term in centre with NICU and pediatric facilites to handle the problem. DETAILED STUDY OF FETAL HEART DOES NOT REVEAL ANY MALFORMATION DETECTABLE BY ULTRASOUND DURING THIS PERIOD OF GESTATION.  Comment : Mrs. Eswari came to us as second opinion for BPD <3%ile and echogenic foci in left ventricle.  In today's scan the placenta, liquor and fetal activity appeared normal for the period of gestation. No other structural anomaly or marker for chromosomal anomalies was noted on scan. Intracranial structures appeared normal. Assessment of the fetal heart showed an echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle. Detailed fetal echocardiography has shown a structurally normal fetal heart.  Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in left ventricle is commonly seen as a normal variant. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  It was noted that she has not undergone any aneuploidy screening in the pregnancy. In today's scan the fetus appeared structurally normal for the period of gestation and no aneuploidy markers were identified. We have reassured them of the same.   Hence the following options of screening were offered to them: 1. Non invasive Prenatal Screening: It is a screening test performed on cell free fetal DNA extracted from maternal blood and has a sensitivity of 99% for detection of  aneuploidies (Trisomy 21/13/18). However, a screen positive/ "No call" test will need further testing for ascertaining chromosomal normalcy. 2. Quadruple screening test: It is a biochemical screening test for aneuploidies (trisomy 21 and 18). The sensitivity of the test in detection of Downs syndrome (Trisomy 21) is 65-70%. A screen positive test will need further testing for ascertaining chromosomal normalcy.  Absolute chromosomal normalcy in any pregnancy can only be established by direct testing - Amniocentesis.  After counselling, they wanted to dicuss and decide. - If she opts for assesment to review - If they opt for routine care review at 28 weeks for interval growth scan (Appointment fixed on 22/2/25 at 11.30 a.m., First floor, Mediscan, Mylapore)  In case of any queries please contact Ph.No.044-24663168 / 240,  Whatsapp No: 9094533300,  eMail ID : fts@mediscan.org.in

In todays scan,the growth,liquor and activity is normal  Today scan findings and its implications explained to couple.  Fetus shows CPAM right lung. Left lung appears normal. No evidence of hydrops at present. No other major structural anomalies noted for the period of gestation.  CPAM, is a developmental abnormality of the lung. There is usually no chromosomal association with this problem and hence at present no further testing is warranted. Predominently these lesions tend to regress with favourable outcome.The size of the lesion can increase in some cases leading to hydrops.Prognosis in these cases would depend on the progression of the lesion indicator for the same being the CVR(CPAM/Volume ratio). At present the CVR is <1.6 & there are no features of hydrops which indicates lesser risk of imminent problem.  We have seen that these lesions usually plateau by third trimester and may not increase in size further and hence close monitoring in this period is essential. This pregnancy would need to be monitored after 2 weeks for that time period to look for 1. CPAM volume 2. features of hydrops 3. Hydramnios.  We have noted that there is a history of cardiac anomalies in previous two pregnancies for which no further workup was done. Hence a detailed fetal echocardiography is suggested at 21-22 weeks.  We have also explained the postnatal implications of CPAM. Lesions that regress/ not being large & no hydrops, generally are associated with favourable outcomes. Some babies would need postnatal management of the lesion .That would depend on findings on followup visits & postnatal picture  Suggested: 1. Repeat scan for target scan, reassessment of the lung lesion and fetal echocardiography after 2 weeks for reassessment. (Appointment fixed on 23.01.2025  at  9.30  am , FMU , Mediscan , Mylapore)  2. Detailed fetal echocardiography has shown a structurally normal fetal heart.  Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in left ventricle is commonly seen as a normal variant. Rest of the fetus appears structurally normal for the period of gestation.   The etiology for multicystic dysplastic kidney can be mutifactorial ,genetic/chromosomal abnormalities. Hidden anomalies of upper and lower GIT , genital abnormalities cannot be identified by antenatal ultrasound and requires postnatal evaluation.  Suggested: 1. Comment: Patient Mrs. Fathima faiza has been referred to us for target scan in  view of echogenic foci in left ventricle and right choroid plexus cyst.  In today's scan the placenta, liquor and fetal activity appeared normal for the period of gestation. Assessment of the fetal heart showed an echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle. Detailed fetal echocardiography has shown a structurally normal  heart. No major anomalies detected in today scan.  Isolated echogenic intracardiac focus in left ventricle is commonly seen as a normal variant. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  Isolated choroid plexus cyst is an innocous finding which is known to regress spontaneously.   It was also noted that the patient had not undergone first trimester biochemical screening    . Hence suggested the option of  Non invasive screening ( NIS) :-This test has a sensitivity of 99% for screening for Downs syndrome. This is a screening test done from cell free fetal DNA in maternal blood and any positive report / " no call " report has to be confirmed by amniocentesis  If the patient opted for testing to review at earliest. If the patient opted out of testing, review at 28 weeks for interval growth scan.

Obstetric history :  G2P1L1 Gravida : 1- 2019 - FTND - female - birth weight -  2.8  kg - alive and healthy  Gravida : 2  - Present Pregnancy   Pedigree highlights : Maternal aunt deaf and dumb  Aneuploidy screening : First Trimester Screening done on  10/1/2025  - HCGb -  0.8  MoM, PAPPa -  0.8  MoM, NT -  0.8  MoM, Nasal bone seen , T21/13/18  -  1:100000 - low risk   Mrs.Saranya has come for routine target scan. The scan findings have been explained to patient and her parents. In todays scan fetal growth,liquor and activity was normal.Extended neurosonogram was done which revealed agenesis of corpus callosum.Rest of the fetus was structurally normal for period of gestation. Agenesis of corpus callosum can be present as an isolated entity or it can be a part of chromosomal abnormality or single gene disorders.  We suggest for fetal MRI and direct testing by Amniocentesis for QFPCR/CMA/DNA store. Storage of fetal DNA would help in future assesment if needed. The pros, cons, cost, turn around time has been explained. Single gene disorders in its entire spectrum cannot be identified by prenatal scans, hence suggested postnatal evaluation.  The prognosis depends upon the etiology and the presence of associated problems. Few associated problems may be detected on follow up visits or sometimes postnatally only.Post natally confirmed cases of isolated ACC may exhibit subtle to moderately severe neurological sequelae (developmental delay/coordination problem) in about 10 - 20 % of cases, with normal outcome in the remainder (70 - 80%). The neurological sequelae cannot be predicted with accuracy at present.   Suggested: 1)Fetal MRI 2)Direct testing by amniocentesis for QFPCR/CMA/DNA storage 3)If they opt for followup, review at 28 weeks 4)In the event of fetal /perinatal demise,  we would suggest that the fetus and placenta can be sent in 10 % formalin (9 parts of water and 1 part of formalin) for a detailed autopsy and HPE (Ph.No.044-24663262/162).This will help us in confirming the diagnosis as well as to rule out other anomalies that may not be evident on ultrasound. In todays scan,the growth,liquor and activity is normal.  Today scan findings explained to couple   Detailed fetal echocardiography shows the presence of Aberrant Right Subclavian artery. Rest of the intracardiac anatomy appears normal. No other soft markers are noted on today's scan.  ARSA as such is not a cardiac defect and does not cause symptoms postnatally in the presence of normal aortic arch. It remains a benign entity in majority remaining as a population variant, but in few cases it can be a marker for Trisomy 21.  Patinet has done first trimester screening shows low risk for T21(1:18620) . This test has a 85% sensitivity for prediction of Down syndrome.However absolute chromosomal normalcy can be ascertained only by direct testing.  Following are the screening and testing options for the common aneuploidies: 1. Non invasive screening ( NIS) :-This test has a sensitivity of 99% for screening for Downs syndrome. This is a screening test done from cell free fetal DNA in maternal blood and any positive report / " no call " report has to be confirmed by amniocentesis  2.Direct Testing : The definitive chromosomal normalcy can be ascertained only by direct testing amniocentesis for QF-PCR and fetal karyotype. The QF-PCR result will be available in one week and karyotype result will be available in 4 weeks. The pros,cons and risk of the direct testing have been explained.  After counselling , patient opted for Direct testing and the same has been done today. Detailed fetal echocardiography done does not reveal any major cardiac malformation. Fetus shows features suggestive of Congenital pulmonary airway malformation - right lung with mediastinal shift to left.CVR is 1.09 (<1.6 - low risk of hydrops). Left lung appears normal. No evidence of hydrops at present.  CPAM, is a developmental abnormality of the lung . There are no known chromosomal association with this isolated CPAM and hence at present no further testing is warranted. Predominently these lesions tend to regress with favourable outcome.The size of the lesion can increase in some cases leading to hydrops.Prognosis in these cases would depend on the progression of the lesion  indicator for the same being the CVR(CPAM/Volume ratio). At present the CVR is <1.6 & there are no features of hydrops which indicates lesser risk of imminent problem. Follow up scans are needs to monitor for 1. CPAM  volume 2. features of hydrops 3. Hydramnios.   These lesions usually plateau by third trimester and may not increase in size further and hence two weekly monitoring till 28weeks is essential.  Lesions that regress/ not being large & no hydrops, generally are associated with favourable outcomes. Some babies would need postnatal management of the lesion .That  would depend on findings on  followup visits & postnatal picture.  An antenatal consultation with pediatric surgeon can be planned at a later date which will help in understanding the postnatal course and management.  Suggested 1.Repeat scan at 2 weeks for reassessment  (Appointment fixed on 09/09/25, at 11.00 pm , FMU, Mediscan, Mylapore) 2.To do ICT at 28weeks  In case of any queries, you could contact us at 9094021681, eMail ID : - fmu@mediscan.org.in ( Monday to Saturday 10.00 a.m. - 4.00 p.m.)

There were no other soft markers noted in today's scan.  Unossified nasal bone could be a normal variant or could be a marker for chromosomal aneuploidy like Down's syndrome. This will increase the apriori risk for aneuploidy by 6 fold. They have done a first trimester combined screening which provides a low risk for trisomy 21 (1:100000), and nasal bone included as present for risk calculation. However, this screening test cannot be considered valid at present. The rationale, interpretation of the screening test and utility of nasal bone in risk calculation have been explained to the couple.  Isolated echogenic intracardiac foci in ventricles is commonly seen as a normal variant. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population. In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac foci, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  In view of unossified nasal bone suggested:  Direct testing by Amniocentesis for QFPCR/KT:  Absolute chromosomal normalcy can only be established by direct testing (amniocentesis for Karyotype and QFPCR ). The procedure, benefits, risks involved were explained in detail to the couple. QFPCR report will be available in one week and karyotype report in 3-4 weeks.  We have also explained that in chromosomally normal fetuses, unossified nasal bone will not have any postnatal implications. In today's scan, it has been noted that fetus B has selective fetal growth restriction stage II(sFGR). Doppler waveforms shows Persistently absent end diastolic flow in the umbilical artery and high resistance flow in the Ductus venosus. Evaluation of fetus B was technically difficult due to oligohydramnios. Growth of fetus A appears normal.   Fetus A (Recipient) - Mild Polyhydramnios, Bladder seen, normal growth, Doppler waveforms normal. Fetus B (Donor) - Severe Oligohydramnios, Bladder seen, Stage II sFGR. --- TWIN TO TWIN TRANSFUSION SYNDROME - STAGE 1  The above changes are due to the changes in the placental vascular anastomosis, shared in common placenta in monochorionic twins. Any imbalance in the blood flow in these blood vessels leads to discordancy in the liquor, bladder and growth. If left untreated, this condition can progress and can potentially lead to cardiac failure in the recipient twin , IUFD of donor twin and with potential loss of both twins.  Considering the above situation we have discussed the following management option -   Selective fetal reduction of fetus B  by microwave ablation - There is an 80 % chance of co twin survival rate and a 15 - 20 % chance of complications such as preterm lsbor / Preterm premature rupture of membranes, bleeding pv, intrauterine fetal demise and co twin anemia. The procedure has been explained. Serial follow up of fetus A will be required post procedure. This is done to monitor the anatomy, liquor, growth and dopplers. In the event normal antenatalfollow up, delivery can be planned at 36 weeks. In the event of preterm labor, chance of NICU care has been advised.  The above findings have been explained to the couple in detail.   After counselling, they have opted for selective fetal reduction by microwave ablation. By the second trimester of pregnancy, however, the organs of the baby have been formed and birth defects from MMI are less likely. Isolated echogenic intracardiac foci in both ventricles is commonly seen as a normal variant. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly. Extent of cleft could not be asseseed properly due to advanced gestation and fetal position.Rest of the fetus appeared structurally normal for period of gestation upto visulised extent.Detailed fetal echo reveals no abnormality.   The etiology of facial clefts is predominantly multifactorial (maternal diabetes /chromosomal /genetic causes). In some cases, it can be associated with single gene disorder or chromosomal abnormality. She has done first trimester combined screening for common aneuploidies which is low risk. However absolute chromosomal normalcy can be ascertained by direct testing only. Single gene disorders can be ruled out postnatally only. Due to advanced gestation we have suggested for routine follow up and postnatal evaluation.  Cleft lip and palate , if isolated , is a potentially correctable problem that requires postnatal treatment. This fetus would  require  supportive treatment, palatal obturators and step-wise surgeries for correction of the cleft lip and palate as well as orthodontic treatment. The nature and timing of the surgeries and long term outcomes  would depend upon the findings at postnatal evaluation by a maxillofacial surgeon and team .  We need to look for any other evolving findings / Polyhydramnios in subsequent scans. Certain occult anomalies of the GIT(Imperforate anus / Tracheoesophageal fistula) cannot be detected on ultrasound.  USG evalution was difficult and hence patient was asked to review for followup scan after 2 days for further  counseling. Assessment of the fetal heart showed an echogenic intracardiac focus in the left ventricle. Detailed fetal echocardiography has shown a structurally normal fetal heart. It is thought to be seen more frequently in asian population.In isolation, an echogenic intracardiac focus, is a weak marker for aneuploidy ( Likelihood ratio:0.95) and does not increase the background risk for aneuploidy, significantly.  Single umbilical artery can be associated with GIT,GUT , cardiac & growth problems.  Both kidneys appear normal for the period of gestation. However, a small percentage of  fetus with single umbilical artery may be associated with occult anomalies (obstruction of upper / lower gastrointestinal tract) which cannot be detected by ultrasound, hence suggested postnatal clinical evaluation.  Suggested repeat scan after 8 weeks to assess the interval growth.

Obstetric history :   Gravida 1 - 2015 - FNTD - Male - B.wt 3.45 kg - Alive and healthy Gravida 2 - 2016 - at 45 days - Spontaneous miscarrige  Gravida 3 - 2017 - at 45 days - Spontaneous miscarrige  Gravida 4 - 2018 - at 45 days - Spontaneous miscarrige  Gravida 5 - 2024 - TOP at 13 weeks for Meckel gruber syndrome  Gravida 6 - Present pregnancy  Comment: Mrs Deepa Singaram came for second opinion for Increased NT and reduced ossification of cranial bones. In today's scan , we have noted above mentioned findings,suggestive of Meckel Gruber Syndrome.This is recurrence of similar features she had in previous pregnancy.
